title: Chocolate sandwich cake with chocolate buttercream
subtitle: Two-layer chocolate sponge filled and covered in dark chocolate buttercream.
excerpt: A chocolate sandwich cake is two light cocoa sponge layers sandwiched together with chocolate buttercream. The sponge uses cocoa powder rather than melted chocolate for a fine crumb. The buttercream is dark, intense, and slightly bitter against the sweet sponge. The whole cake takes under two hours to make.
sourceNotes: The all-in-one or creaming method chocolate sponge is a British baking staple, appearing in household cookbooks from the mid-twentieth century onwards. Using cocoa powder rather than melted chocolate in sponge cakes became standard practice as cocoa powder became widely available. The buttercream sandwich format follows the Victoria sponge structure adapted for chocolate, documented in sources from Delia Smith to the Women's Institute handbooks. This recipe follows the classic British method.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfrosted sponge layers separately, wrapped in cling film. Defrost at room temperature before assembling.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The assembled cake keeps at room temperature for two days, covered loosely. The sponge layers can be baked a day ahead and stored wrapped.

recipeTools.notes: Two 20 cm sandwich tins, greased and lined.
Or a hand mixer. For both sponge and buttercream.

content.text: A chocolate sandwich cake is simply a Victoria sponge with cocoa powder added to the flour and a chocolate buttercream instead of jam and plain cream. The sponge is light because cocoa is used instead of melted chocolate; melted chocolate makes a denser crumb. The buttercream is dark and rich because it uses both cocoa powder and a little melted dark chocolate.
Heat the oven to 170°C fan (190°C conventional). Grease and line two 20 cm sandwich tins.
Beat {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} until very pale and fluffy, about 3 to 4 minutes. Beat in {{eggs}} one at a time.

Sift {{self-raising-flour}}, {{cocoa-powder}}, and {{baking-powder}} into a bowl and set aside. Fold this into the butter mixture in two batches, alternating with {{whole-milk}}. Stop when the flour just disappears.
Divide between the two tins and level. Bake for 28 to 32 minutes until springy to the touch and just pulling away from the sides. Cool in the tins for 10 minutes, then turn onto a rack.

Beat {{unsalted-butter}} alone for 2 minutes until very pale. Sift in {{icing-sugar}} and {{cocoa-powder}} in three additions, beating well between each. Add {{dark-chocolate}} and {{whole-milk}} and beat for a further 3 minutes until smooth and light.
Place one sponge on a board or plate. Spread with about a third of the buttercream. Place the second sponge on top and use the remaining buttercream to cover the top and sides.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: This is a reliable cake with predictable failure modes.

items.cause: Butter or eggs too cold, or overmixed after adding flour.
items.fix: Bring butter and eggs to room temperature before starting. Fold the flour in gently; the moment no dry flour remains, stop.
Sift the cocoa before adding and beat for longer. A tablespoon of hot water added gradually can help smooth it.
